name: Mathematical Induction
tagline: Prove a statement holds for all integers >= some base by proving the base case and showing 'P(n) implies P(n+1).' Strong induction allows P(1)..P(n) to imply P(n+1).

blocks.text: Mathematical induction is the dominoes-falling principle: if you can knock over the first domino (the base case) and you can show that any falling domino knocks over the next (the inductive step), then ALL dominoes fall. Formally: prove P(1), then prove that for all n, P(n) implies P(n+1). Conclusion: P holds for every positive integer.

The variant 'strong induction' lets you assume P(1), P(2), ..., P(n) when proving P(n+1) -- not just the immediate predecessor. This is essential when the inductive step splits into smaller subproblems of arbitrary sizes (e.g., Coin Split, where N coins split into x and N-x for any 1 <= x < N).

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Two questions: (1) Is the base case TRUE? (2) Does the inductive hypothesis ENABLE the next step? If both are yes, the proof writes itself. If you can't prove P(n+1) from P(n), try strong induction. If even that fails, the statement may be wrong.

whenToUse: Proving a formula or property holds for ALL positive integers.
Proving an algorithm produces the same output regardless of intermediate choices.
Bounding the worst case of a recursive process.
When the structure of the problem naturally splits into smaller versions of itself.
recipe: State the claim P(n) precisely. Vague claims lead to vague induction.
Verify the base case P(1) -- or whatever the smallest meaningful n is.
Assume P(n) (or P(1)..P(n) for strong induction). Prove P(n+1).
When the inductive step uses subproblems of arbitrary smaller size, you need STRONG induction.
Always check that the inductive step uses the hypothesis -- otherwise you might be proving the wrong thing.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to check the base case. (A famous 'proof' that all horses are the same color fails because the base case n=1 doesn't compose to n=2.)
Inductive step that doesn't actually use P(n) -- this isn't induction, it's a direct proof.
Using P(n+1) inside the proof of P(n+1) (circular reasoning).
Confusing 'P(n) for all n' with 'P holds in the limit' -- induction proves the former, not the latter.
Failing to verify that the inductive step works for ALL n, including edge cases like n=1 -> n=2.
